messages.content: To quantify the amount of acid sites over the reduced catalysts, NH3-TPD analysis was performed.

NH3-TPD, or ammonia temperature programmed desorption analysis, is a common technique used to quantify the amount of acid sites present on a catalyst or material. This method involves dosing ammonia onto the sample and then heating it up gradually. As the temperature increases, the ammonia desorbs from the surface of the catalyst, and the amount of ammonia desorbing at different temperatures can be used to characterize the acid sites on the catalyst.

By analyzing the NH3-TPD data, researchers can determine the total number of acid sites present on the catalyst, as well as the strength and distribution of these acid sites. This information is essential for understanding the catalytic activity and selectivity of the catalyst, as well as for optimizing its performance in various chemical reactions.

Overall, NH3-TPD analysis provides valuable insights into the acid-base properties of catalysts and is a powerful tool for catalyst design and optimization.

NH3-TPD analysis was employed to quantify the number of acid sites present on the reduced catalysts.

Kong et al. [51] studied a series of Ni/Al2O3 catalysts derived from hydrotalcite-like compounds and concluded that the balance Ni-acid sites facilitate the DMF production, while excessive metal sites would promote hydrogenation of C=C bonds to form DHMTHF.

In their study, Kong et al. [51] examined a range of Ni/Al2O3 catalysts obtained from hydrotalcite-like compounds and determined that an appropriate balance of Ni-acid sites enhances DMF production, while an excess of metal sites promotes the hydrogenation of C=C bonds leading to the formation of DHMTHF.
Altogether, 450 oC was selected as an optimal reduction temperature for Ni, Co, and CoNi catalysts as it practically guarantees the vast majority of metallic oxide species.
In total, a reduction temperature of 450 oC was found to be optimal for Ni, Co, and CoNi catalysts as it ensures the conversion of the majority of metallic oxide species.

The H2-TPR measurements was used for estimating the optimal reduction temperature and to obtain information about the reducibility of supported Ni precursor, which depends on the degree of interaction with the support

H2-TPR measurements were utilized to determine the ideal reduction temperature and to gather details on the reducibility of the supported Ni precursor, which is influenced by the extent of interaction with the support.

After deposition of the metal precursor on the supports, the samples were dried in air at room temperature and then at 100 oC overnight. Prior to the catalytic tests, the samples were grinded and sieved to a particle size ≤250 μm in order to avoid internal mass transfer limitations. Before the characterization (except for TPR profiles) and the catalytic tests, the samples were reduced under pure hydrogen for 2 hours at 300 oC for Cu/HSAG and CoCu/HSAG and 450 oC in the case of Ni-based catalysts (rate 5 oC/min).

Following the deposition of the metal precursor onto the supports, the samples underwent air-drying at room temperature and then at 100 oC overnight. Subsequently, the samples were ground and sieved to a particle size of ≤250 μm to prevent any internal mass transfer limitations. Prior to both characterization (excluding TPR profiles) and the catalytic assessments, the samples were subjected to reduction under pure hydrogen for 2 hours at 300 oC for Cu/HSAG and CoCu/HSAG, and at 450 oC for Ni-based catalysts (at a heating rate of 5 oC/min).

The textural properties of carbon and metal oxides supported Ni catalysts, such as specific surface area and pore size, were measured by nitrogen physisorption using a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 instrument. Prior to N2 adsorption, the catalysts were outgassed in vacuum at 423 K for 12 h. The specific surface area was calculated by the Brunauer– Emmett–Teller (BET) equation and pore size (dBJH) was determined by the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda equation (BJH) from the desorption isotherm. The internal and external surface of the AC was obtained by applying t-plot method.

The textural characteristics of nickel catalysts supported by carbon and metal oxides, such as specific surface area and pore size, were evaluated using nitrogen physisorption with a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 instrument. Before nitrogen adsorption, the catalysts underwent vacuum outgassing at 423 K for 12 hours. The specific surface area was calculated using the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) equation, while the pore size (dBJH) was determined via the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) equation from the desorption isotherm. The internal and external surface of the activated carbon were analyzed using the t-plot method.
Thermogravimetric analyses (TGA) were carried out in an SDT Q 600 apparatus for both the reduced and spent catalysts, under inert (He, 100 mL/min) and air atmosphere (100 mL/min), respectively.
Thermogravimetric analyses (TGA) were conducted using an SDT Q 600 apparatus for both the reduced and spent catalysts, under inert (He, 100 mL/min) and air atmosphere (100 mL/min), respectively.

The reduction behavior of the metal precursor and its interaction with the support was evaluated by Temperature Programed Reduction (TPR). The reduction profiles were obtained using a U-shaped quartz reactor with 0.15 g of sample. The catalyst was heated from room temperature to 700 oC with a 10 oC /min rate under 35 mL/min of a mixture of 5 vol.% hydrogen in argon (Air Liquide 99.999% purity).

The reduction process of the metal precursor and its interaction with the support were assessed through Temperature Programmed Reduction (TPR). Reduction profiles were generated utilizing a U-shaped quartz reactor containing 0.15 g of the sample. The catalyst underwent heating from room temperature to 700 oC at a rate of 10 oC/min under a flow of 35 mL/min of a mixture comprising 5 vol.% hydrogen in argon (Air Liquide 99.999% purity).

The catalyst was heated from room temperature to 700 oC with a 10 oC /min rate under 35 mL/min of a mixture of 5 vol.% hydrogen in argon (Air Liquide 99.999% purity).

The reduction characteristics of the metal precursor and its interaction with the support were analyzed through Temperature Programmed Reduction (TPR). TPR profiles were acquired using a U-shaped quartz reactor containing 0.15 g of the sample. The catalyst underwent heating from ambient temperature to 700 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min under a flow of 35 mL/min of a 5 vol.% hydrogen mixture in argon (Air Liquide, 99.999% purity).

The reduction profiles were obtained using a U-shaped quartz reactor with 0.15 g of sample. The catalyst was heated from room temperature to 700 oC with a 10 oC /min rate under 35 mL/min of a mixture of 5 vol.% hydrogen in argon (Air Liquide 99.999% purity).

The reduction characteristics of the metal precursor and its interaction with the support were assessed through Temperature Programmed Reduction (TPR). Reduction profiles were obtained using a U-shaped quartz reactor containing 0.15 g of the sample. The catalyst was subjected to heating from room temperature to 700°C at a rate of 10°C/min under a flow of 35 mL/min of a 5 vol.% hydrogen mixture in argon (Air Liquide, 99.999% purity).

The crystallinity of the Ni catalysts and supports was characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD), which was measured with a Polycristal X’Pert Pro PANalytical diffractometer operating at 45 kV and 40 mA using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1542 Å) with the 2θ range of 4–90° and the scan speed of 0.04 o/s.

The crystalline structure of the Ni catalysts and supports was analyzed using powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements conducted with a Polycrystal X’Pert Pro PANalytical diffractometer. The XRD analysis was performed at 45 kV and 40 mA with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1542 Å), covering a 2θ range of 4-90° at a scan speed of 0.04 °/s.

The size and dispersion of Ni particles over the different supports were studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images by means of a JEOL JEM-2100 field-emission gun electron microscope operated at 200 kV. The samples were ground and ultrasonically suspended in ethanol before TEM images were produced. The average particle size of Ni (dTEM) was calculated, based on a minimum of 400 particles, using the following formula: dTEM =∑nidi3/∑nidi2, being ni the number of particles with a diameter of di. X-ray energy dispersive spectroscopic (EDS) mapping analysis was obtained with an INCA X-sight detector (Oxford Instruments). A spot size of 0.25 nm was employed for the scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) analysis.

The size and distribution of Ni particles on various supports were investigated through transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images using a JEOL JEM-2100 field-emission gun electron microscope operating at 200 kV. Prior to TEM imaging, the samples were ground and ultrasonically dispersed in ethanol. The average particle size of Ni (dTEM) was determined based on a minimum of 400 particles using the formula: dTEM = ∑nidi3 / ∑nidi2, where ni represents the number of particles with a diameter of di. Additionally, X-ray energy dispersive spectroscopic (EDS) mapping analysis was conducted with an INCA X-sight detector from Oxford Instruments. A spot size of 0.25 nm was utilized for the scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) analysis.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were collected with an ESCA-PROBE P (Omicron) spectrometer by using non-monochromatized Mg-Kα radiation (1253.6 eV). Prior to the analysis, each sample was pressed into a small pellet of 10 mm diameter, placed in the sample holder, and degassed in the chamber for 24 h to achieve a dynamic vacuum below 10-10 mbar. The spectral data for each sample were analyzed using CASA XPS software. The binding energy (BE) was referenced to the C 1s peak at 284.6 eV.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were acquired using an ESCA-PROBE P (Omicron) spectrometer with non-monochromatized Mg-Kα radiation (1253.6 eV). Before the analysis, each sample was compressed into a small pellet measuring 10 mm in diameter, positioned in the sample holder, and degassed in the chamber for 24 hours to attain a dynamic vacuum level below 10-10 mbar. The spectral data for each sample were processed using CASA XPS software. The binding energy (BE) was calibrated against the C 1s peak at 284.6 eV.

Before the analysis, each sample was compressed into a small pellet measuring 10 mm in diameter, positioned in the sample holder, and degassed in the chamber for 24 hours to attain a dynamic vacuum level below 10-10 mbar. The spectral data for each sample were processed using CASA XPS software. The binding energy (BE) was calibrated against the C 1s peak at 284.6 eV.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained using an ESCA-PROBE P (Omicron) spectrometer with non-monochromatized Mg-Kα radiation (1253.6 eV). Prior to the analysis, each sample was compressed into a small pellet measuring 10 mm in diameter, placed in the sample holder, and degassed in the chamber for 24 hours to achieve a dynamic vacuum level below 10-10 mbar. The spectral data for each sample were analyzed using CASA XPS software, and the binding energy (BE) was calibrated using the C 1s peak at 284.6 eV.

The number of acid sites was determined by means of temperature programmed desorption (TPD) of ammonia (NH3), using a Micromeritics Autochem II 2920 instrument coupled to a thermal conductivity detector (TCD). Prior to the analysis, the pre-reduced catalyst (ca. 150 mg) was reduced in-situ under H2 flow at 450 °C for 1 h. After cooling the sample to 100 oC, it was saturated with a 5% NH3/He flow for 1 h at 100 °C. Next, He was allowed to pass over the material at 100 °C for 1 h to remove physiosorbed NH3. Later, the temperature was increased up to 800 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min-1 and the release of NH3 was monitored using a TCD detector. A blank test was performed following the same steps, except for saturation with the analysis gas, in order to quantify the possible desorption products from the surface of the samples, which were subtracted from the monitored NH3 in the TPD experiments.

The amount of acid sites was determined through temperature programmed desorption (TPD) of ammonia (NH3) using a Micromeritics Autochem II 2920 instrument equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD). Prior to the analysis, the pre-reduced catalyst (approximately 150 mg) was reduced in-situ under an H2 flow at 450 °C for 1 hour. After cooling the sample to 100 °C, it was exposed to a 5% NH3/He flow for 1 hour at 100 °C. Subsequently, helium (He) was passed over the material at 100 °C for 1 hour to eliminate physically adsorbed NH3. The temperature was then raised to 800 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min, and the release of NH3 was monitored using a TCD detector. A control test was carried out following the same steps, excluding saturation with the analysis gas, to quantify any desorption products from the sample surface, which were subtracted from the NH3 released during the TPD experiments.
As extensively reported in literature, a bifunctional catalyst with both metal and Lewis’s acid sites, offering hydrogenation and deoxygenation abilities, respectively, is required
As widely documented in the literature, a bifunctional catalyst possessing both metal and Lewis acid sites is essential, as it provides hydrogenation and deoxygenation capabilities, respectively.
